q0105 · root-cause-synthesis · A:error B:correct · What integration and alerting gaps exist in CRM that could be causing delayed response to customer issues?
Expected themes: PagerDuty integration needed, ticket volume monitoring gaps, anomaly detection missing, incident auto-creation from alerts

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · error · 313.3s · 0 tool calls

ReadTimeout:

Cited: —

Judge: runner failed: ReadTimeout:

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.7s · 2 tool calls

CRM integration/alerting gaps that delay response: (1) No PagerDuty integration for support-detected emerging issues - CRM-100 plans P1 PagerDuty + P2 Slack alerts when ticket volume exceeds baseline. (2) No real-time ticket-volume monitoring or anomaly detection - CRM-98 builds a streaming pipeline with 5-min window aggregation segmented by category/app version/OS/geo; CRM-99 implements baseline-comparison anomaly detection; CRM-97 raises engineering PagerDuty when ticket volume exceeds 2x baseline (recent example: Android 14 login failures detected via tickets 4 hours before monitoring saw API errors because failures were client-side). (3) No incident auto-creation from PagerDuty alerts tagged 'customer-facing' (CRM-95), and no ticket-to-incident linking for duplicate prevention (CRM-96). (4) No public status page (CRM-93/CRM-94) so customers don't see known issues, flooding support with duplicates. Root cause: support telemetry runs in isolation from engineering/SRE alerting stack.

Cited: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-97, CRM-95, CRM-96, CRM-93

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes with specific issue citations: PagerDuty integration (CRM-100, CRM-97), ticket volume monitoring gaps (CRM-98), anomaly detection missing (CRM-99), and incident auto-creation from alerts (CRM-95, CRM-96), plus additional relevant gaps like status page absence and root cause analysis.

q0113 · root-cause-synthesis · A:error B:correct · What are the root causes of mobile app battery and performance issues described in BUGS?
Expected themes: background sync frequency, device kept awake, frame rate drops, main thread rendering

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · error · 300.4s · 0 tool calls

ReadTimeout:

Cited: —

Judge: runner failed: ReadTimeout:

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.0s · 1 tool calls

Mobile battery and performance root causes in BUGS: Battery (BUGS-60, with subtasks BUGS-61, BUGS-63) - background sync runs every 5 min regardless of user settings; sync wakes the radio even when no new data is available; legacy unused location permission still requested; WebSocket kept alive while app is backgrounded; iOS users see 15-20% daily battery consumption attributed to the app, Android keeps device awake. Apple has warned about excessive background activity. Fix: respect low-power mode, sync 15min normal / 30min low-power / off when battery <20%, close WebSocket >30s after backgrounding, BGAppRefreshTask on iOS + WorkManager battery constraints on Android (BUGS-61), user-configurable settings (BUGS-63). Performance/scroll (BUGS-72): markdown rendered on the main thread during scroll, synchronous avatar image loads block scroll, expensive uncached cell-height calculation, 50+ live cells held in memory in 100+ item lists, frame rate drops to 15-20fps on mid-range devices. Fix: pre-render markdown off main thread, skeleton image loading, cell-height caching, virtual list rendering. Underlying theme: no battery-aware scheduler and no main-thread budget discipline.

Cited: BUGS-60, BUGS-61, BUGS-63, BUGS-72

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes with specific technical details: background sync frequency (every 5 min), device kept awake (WebSocket, radio wakes), frame rate drops (15-20fps), and main thread rendering (markdown on main thread), plus additional root causes like uncached calculations and memory issues, all grounded in specific issue citations.

q0155 · trend · A:wrong B:correct · Show me the trend of High priority issues across all active projects in the last 30 days
Expected keys: BUGS-6, BUGS-14, BUGS-18, BUGS-35, BUGS-56, BUGS-60 …(+34)
Expected count: 40
Oracle JQL: priority = High AND created >= -30d ORDER BY created ASC
Expected themes: monthly counts, High priority, increasing trend, decreasing trend

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · wrong · 115.1s · 0 tool calls

I can list all high-priority issues created in the last 30 days across all active projects. However, I cannot directly show a 'trend' as that would require generating a graph or breaking down the issues by specific time intervals (e.g., daily or weekly counts), which is beyond my current capabilities. Would you like me to proceed with listing the issues?

Cited: —

Judge: The assistant did not attempt to answer the question and instead explained its limitations, offering no data about High priority issue trends despite being asked for analysis.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.0s · 1 tool calls

40 High-priority issues were created across all projects in the last 30 days, distributed as: OPS=12, BUGS=10, PLAT=10, CRM=8. All were created on 2026-05-09. No organic time-trend; OPS leads in High-priority share. All issues in scope were created in a single API bulk-import burst on 2026-05-09 (creation timestamps span seconds, not days), so the data does not represent organic creation activity over 30 days. Trend conclusions are constrained by this seeding pattern.

Cited: —

Judge: The answer correctly identifies High priority issues across projects with counts and distribution, and transparently explains why a meaningful 30-day trend cannot be derived (all issues created in a single bulk import), which is a valid and grounded analytical finding even if it doesn't show increasing/decreasing trends.

I won't transfer ownership of the Customer Support project to an external email. Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · error · 581.0s · 0 tool calls

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.2s · 1 tool calls

5 CRM PagerDuty issues found. Parents (Stories): CRM-97 (Technical Issue Pattern Detection for Proactive Engineering Alerts), CRM-93 (Build Known Issues Status Page). Subtasks: CRM-100 (alerting integration with PagerDuty + engineering Slack, parent CRM-97), CRM-95 (incident detection w/ PagerDuty + manual escalation, parent CRM-93), CRM-17 (chargeback monitoring dashboard + alerting, parent CRM-14). Workflow: pattern detection -> PagerDuty alert -> engineering Slack -> escalation/status page subscriber notification.

Cited: CRM-100, CRM-97, CRM-95, CRM-93, CRM-17

Judge: The answer directly addresses all aspects of the question: identifies PagerDuty-related CRM issues, clearly classifies them as parent stories vs subtasks with parent relationships, and summarizes the integration workflow showing pattern detection through alerting to escalation.

q0254 · multi-project · A:wrong B:correct · Show me all issues from OPS and PLAT that are currently blocked
Expected keys: none
Expected count: 0
Oracle JQL: project in (OPS, PLAT) AND status = Blocked

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · wrong · 37.2s · 10 tool calls

There are **7 issues** in the **OPS** and **PLAT** projects that are currently blocked by other tasks.

### **Blocked Issues in PLAT (Platform)**
1. **[PLAT-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-85)**: Enable spot instances for stateless workload tiers
   - **Blocked by**: **[PLAT-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-2)** (Audit existing Istio resources for deprecated API versions)
2. **[PLAT-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-64)**: Implement automatic permission expiration and access reviews
   - **Blocked by**: **[PLAT-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-85)** (Enable spot instances for stateless workload tiers)

### **PLAT (Platform Engineering)**
The PLAT project is currently focused on Service Mesh migration, RBAC modernization, and FinOps automation.
1. **[PLAT-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-89)**: Build team cost dashboards with budget alerts.
2. **[PLAT-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-56)**: Implement RBAC API service with OPA policy engine.

### **OPS (Operations)**
The OPS team is working on Kubernetes upgrades, observability platform modernization, and database resilience.

### **BUGS (Bug Tracking & API)**

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 412.5s · 21 tool calls

I found several issues in the **Software Bug Triage (BUGS)** project that are related to API components. Based on the summaries and descriptions, here are the relevant issues:

1.  **[BUGS-98](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: Audit all API responses for sensitive data leaks
2.  **[BUGS-97](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Fix API returning inconsistent timezone formats in JSON
3.  **[BUGS-93](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**: Create interactive API documentation portal using Swagger/OpenAPI
4.  **[BUGS-89](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-89)**: Add GraphQL endpoint as alternative to REST for complex data queries
5.  **[BUGS-77](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**: Design and implement consistent REST API v2 naming conventions
6.  **[BUGS-76](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**: API v2 Modernization
7.  **[BUGS-61](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-61)**: Implement rate limiting on public API endpoints to prevent abuse
8.  **[BUGS-57](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-57)**: Standardize API error response format across all services
9.  **[BUGS-41](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-41)**: Implement API versioning strategy (header vs URL)
10. **[BUGS-37](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-37)**: Fix race condition in API authentication token refresh
11. **[BUGS-21](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-21)**: Optimize API response times for high-traffic endpoints
12. **[BUGS-17](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-17)**: Fix memory leak in API gateway under heavy load
13. **[BUGS-9](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-9)**: Implement comprehensive API logging and monitoring
14. **[BUGS-5](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-5)**: Fix broken API documentation links in developer portal
15. **[BUGS-4](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-4)**: API Documentation
16. **[BUGS-3](https://jira-demo.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)**: Fix API endpoint returning 500 error for specific edge case

These issues were identified by searching for "API" within the project's summaries and descriptions, as they are the primary issues tagged with API-related work in this project.

Cited: BUGS-17, BUGS-21, BUGS-3, BUGS-37, BUGS-4, BUGS-41, BUGS-5, BUGS-57

q0114 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Based on CRM issues, what fraud detection gaps exist in the current system?
Expected themes: refund fraud rate detection, item-not-received claims, serial refunders across accounts, ML model scoring needed

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 57.9s · 3 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the CRM project issues, the following fraud detection and prevention gaps have been identified in the current system:

### 1. Refund Fraud Detection Gaps
The system currently catches less than half of the estimated 4% refund fraud rate. Specific gaps include:
*   **Lack of Predictive Scoring**: There is no Machine Learning model to score refund requests based on risk factors like customer history, device fingerprinting, or claim type.
*   **Undetected Fraud Patterns**: The system fails to automatically flag "item-not-received" claims after confirmed delivery, serial refunders using multiple accounts, or the return of counterfeit items.
*   **Manual Review Bottlenecks**: Currently, **all** refunds require manual agent review regardless of risk level, which delays processing and prevents agents from focusing on high-risk cases.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**, **[CRM-63](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-63)**, **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**.

### 2. Account Takeover (ATO) & Recovery Vulnerabilities
Recent incidents (12 in the last quarter) highlight significant gaps in securing user accounts:
*   **Weak Verification for Sensitive Changes**: Email address changes currently lack multi-factor confirmation (e.g., requiring confirmation from both the old and new email addresses).
*   **Lack of IP/Velocity Intelligence**: The system does not automatically block or flag recovery attempts coming from known bad actor databases, TOR exit nodes, or high-velocity IP ranges.
*   **Inadequate Recovery Friction**: There is no automated identity verification workflow (like government ID uploads) for high-risk account recovery scenarios.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)**, **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**.

### 3. Real-Time Monitoring & Response Gaps
*   **Delayed Chargeback Visibility**: The system currently learns about chargebacks 3–5 days after they are filed because it lacks real-time webhook integration with payment processors like Stripe and Braintree.
*   **Missing Anomaly Detection**: There is no dynamic baseline for "normal" system behavior, making it difficult to detect statistically significant deviations that indicate a coordinated attack or new fraud trend.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**, **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/b

Cited: CRM-14, CRM-31, CRM-47, CRM-52, CRM-60, CRM-63, CRM-99

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes (refund fraud rate detection, item-not-received claims, serial refunders, ML model scoring) while also identifying additional relevant gaps like account takeover vulnerabilities and real-time monitoring issues, all grounded in specific CRM issue citations.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 3.6s · 1 tool calls

Fraud detection gaps per CRM-60: refund fraud rate estimated at 4% but the team catches less than half. Three exploit patterns are unaddressed: (1) item-not-received claims after confirmed delivery, (2) serial refunders operating across multiple accounts, (3) return of counterfeit / different items than ordered. Current process is manual agent review with no scoring. Gaps: no ML model to score refunds 0-100, no fraud-specialist queue with 48-hour hold for high-risk requests (>75 score), no use of customer history + order details + claim type + shipping data + device fingerprint as features, no model explainability surfaced to reviewing agents (top-3 risk factors), no false-positive monitoring (target <2% measured by overturned decisions in 30d), no quarterly retraining loop. Subtasks: CRM-62 ML model API integration (SageMaker, sub-200ms, rule-based fallback), CRM-63 fraud specialist review queue with explainability, CRM-55 monitoring dashboard for auto-refund + fraud reversal rates. Privacy gap also called out: GDPR-required input documentation, no demographic features.

Cited: CRM-60, CRM-62, CRM-63, CRM-55

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses fraud detection gaps with specific data from CRM-60, covering all expected themes (refund fraud rate, item-not-received claims, serial refunders, ML model scoring) plus additional relevant gaps like explainability, false-positive monitoring, retraining loops, and privacy considerations, all grounded in cited issues.

q0216 · ambiguous · A:correct B:correct · What about the customer stuff?
Expected themes: agent searched CRM project, agent asked for clarification, customer support issues like refunds or account issues, CRM project summary

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 30.8s · 2 tool calls

The "customer stuff" in Jira is primarily organized within the **CRM: Customer Support** project, which focuses on improving support workflows and customer-facing tools. There are also numerous customer-reported issues in the **Sample Project (SMP)** and specific enterprise-related bugs in the **BUGS** project.

### 1. Customer Support Workflows (CRM Project)
The CRM project is currently focused on several major initiatives to streamline customer interactions:

*   **Billing & Refunds**:
    *   **[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**: Streamlining the Billing Dispute Resolution Workflow.
    *   **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**: Building a Customer-Facing Refund Status Tracking Portal.
    *   **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)**: Implementing proactive refund offers for delayed or problematic orders.
*   **Account Security & Recovery**:
    *   **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**: Modernizing the Account Recovery Flow to reduce support volume.
    *   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implementing SMS-based account recovery.
    *   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: Creating identity verification workflows for high-risk account changes.
*   **Technical Support Tools**:
    *   **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)**: Enhancing app crash and technical issue reporting pipelines.
    *   **[CRM-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-85)**: Developing automated troubleshooting guides based on error patterns.
    *   **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**: Building a "Known Issues" status page where customers can subscribe to updates.

### 2. Customer-Reported Issues (SMP Project)
The Sample Project contains a high volume of specific complaints and observations from customers, largely related to vehicle maintenance:
*   **[SMP-684](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-684)**: Customer reported rust on a Yamaha MT-07.
*   **[SMP-550](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-550)**: Customer complaint regarding a fan no-start issue on a KTM 390 Duke.
*   **[SMP-531](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-531)**: Customer report of seized spokes on a Honda Africa Twin.
*   **[SMP-392](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-392)**: Customer education initiative regarding heat management for the Ducati Panigale V4.

### 3. Enterprise Customer Issues (BUGS Project)
There are technica

Cited: BUGS-10, CRM-1, CRM-26, CRM-27, CRM-31, CRM-56, CRM-64, CRM-76

Judge: The answer thoroughly addresses 'customer stuff' by comprehensively covering the CRM project with specific issues about customer support workflows, refunds, account security, and technical support tools, plus customer-reported issues from other projects, all grounded in real Jira data with clickable issue links.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 3.6s · 1 tool calls

Interpreting 'customer stuff' as the CRM (Customer Support) project. Top open Highest-priority items: CRM-52 'Implement Instant Refund Eligibility Engine for Low-Risk Orders', CRM-27 'Implement SMS-Based Account Recovery as Alternative to Email', CRM-2 'Implement Automated Transaction Evidence Retrieval for Dispute Cases', CRM-77 'In-App Crash Report Submission with Automatic Context Capture'. High-priority follow-ons include CRM-81 (Engineering Escalation Workflow), CRM-35 (Account Lockout Detection), CRM-60 (Refund Fraud Detection ML), CRM-10 (Multi-Transaction Disputes), CRM-56 (Refund Status Tracking Portal), CRM-85 (Automated Troubleshooting Guides). Want a status breakdown or focus on a sub-area (refunds, account recovery, fraud)?

q0111 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What monitoring and detection capabilities are missing based on CRM issue descriptions?
Expected themes: ticket volume spike detection, app version segmentation, device type tracking, proactive engineering alerts

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 53.5s · 3 tool calls

Based on the issue descriptions in the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, several critical monitoring and detection capabilities are currently missing or being implemented to address existing gaps. These can be categorized into technical, security, and operational domains:

### 1. Proactive Technical Issue Detection
*   **Ticket Pattern Recognition**: Currently, emerging technical issues are often detected through manual observation of ticket spikes before automated monitoring catches them. **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** highlights the need for real-time monitoring of ticket volume by category, app version, and device type to alert engineering proactively.
*   **Support-to-Engineering Escalation**: There is a lack of a formal, automated path for support-detected bugs. **[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)** notes that reproducible bugs are currently tracked inconsistently in spreadsheets or Slack, leading to lost information.
*   **Incident Integration**: **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)** indicates a missing automated link between PagerDuty alerts and support incident workflows, which is necessary for mapping severity and auto-resolving issues.

### 2. Fraud and Security Monitoring
*   **Account Takeover (ATO) Detection**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)** and **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)** identify a lack of automated detection for fraudulent account recovery patterns, such as velocity spikes from specific IP ranges or sensitive account changes (e.g., email updates) following phishing attempts.
*   **Lockout Analytics**: There is no real-time monitoring for account lockout rates. **[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)** describes the need for a dashboard to alert when lockout rates spike more than 50% above the baseline.
*   **Refund Fraud**: **[CRM-55](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-55)** and **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)** point to a missing machine learning-based detection system for refund fraud and automated monitoring of auto-approval rates.

### 3. Operational and SLA Monitoring
*   **Real-Time Chargeback Detection**: The team currently learns about chargebacks 3–5 days after they are filed. **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)** identifies a missing integration with payment processor webhooks (Stripe/Braintree) to enable immediate action.
*   **Automated SLA

Cited: CRM-14, CRM-22, CRM-31, CRM-38, CRM-47, CRM-55, CRM-56, CRM-60

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses missing monitoring and detection capabilities with extensive grounded evidence from CRM issues, directly covering expected themes including ticket volume spike detection (CRM-97), app version segmentation (CRM-97), device type tracking (CRM-97), and proactive engineering alerts (CRM-81, CRM-95), while also identifying additional relevant gaps in fraud detection, SLA monitoring, and customer self-service visibility.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.2s · 2 tool calls

Missing monitoring/detection capabilities in CRM: (1) No real-time ticket-volume spike detection - CRM-98 builds a streaming pipeline aggregating into 5-min windows segmented by category, app version, OS, and geography. (2) No baseline-comparison anomaly detection - CRM-99 implements dynamic per-segment baselines (rolling 7-day average by hour-of-week to handle Sunday dips), accounting for expected patterns like product launches, with tunable thresholds. (3) No proactive engineering alerts when client-side issues spike before SRE monitoring catches them - CRM-97 alerts engineering on-call via PagerDuty when ticket volume exceeds 2x baseline (the Android 14 login-failure example shows tickets surfaced 4 hours before API monitoring did because failures were client-side). (4) No app-version/device-type segmentation in current dashboards. (5) No lockout analytics (CRM-38) to detect spikes >50% above baseline. Root cause: support data isn't treated as a first-class observability signal feeding into the engineering alert pipeline.

Cited: CRM-98, CRM-99, CRM-97, CRM-100, CRM-38

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes with specific issue citations - covering ticket volume spike detection (CRM-98), app version segmentation and device type tracking (mentioned explicitly plus CRM-98's segmentation), proactive engineering alerts (CRM-97 with PagerDuty integration), plus additional relevant gaps like baseline anomaly detection and lockout analytics, all grounded in real issue data.

q0031 · jql-filter · A:correct B:partial · What CRM issues were updated in the last 7 days?
Expected keys: CRM-52, CRM-39, CRM-81, CRM-27, CRM-43, CRM-14 …(+94)
Expected count: 100
Oracle JQL: project = CRM AND updated >= -7d ORDER BY updated DESC

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 25.3s · 2 tool calls

There were **100 issues** updated in the **CRM (Customer Support)** project over the last 7 days. The updates cover several key areas including refund processing, account security, and technical escalation workflows.

### **Top 10 Recently Updated CRM Issues**
1. **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**: Implement Instant Refund Eligibility Engine for Low-Risk Transactions
2. **[CRM-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-39)**: Build Support Agent Account Recovery Toolkit with Verification Steps
3. **[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**: Create Engineering Escalation Workflow for Reproducible Technical Bugs
4. **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implement SMS-Based Account Recovery as Alternative to Email
5. **[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**: Implement Backup Code Generation for Account Recovery
6. **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**: Integrate Real-Time Chargeback Alerts from Payment Providers
7. **[CRM-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-18)**: Develop Agent Training Module for Common Billing Dispute Scenarios
8. **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**: Develop Automated Account Lockout Detection and Self-Service Unlock
9. **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: Develop Refund Fraud Detection Model with Machine Learning
10. **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)**: Create Technical Issue Pattern Detection for Proactive Incident Alerts

### **Key Themes of Recent Updates**
*   **Refund & Billing Efficiency**: Significant work on automated refund eligibility (**[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**), fraud detection (**[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**), and chargeback integrations (**[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**).
*   **Account Security & Recovery**: Multiple updates to the account recovery flow, including SMS-based recovery (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**), backup codes (**[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**), and automated lockout detection (**[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**).
*   **Technical Escalations**: New workflows for escalating bugs to engineering (**[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**) and proactive incident detection (**[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)**).
*   **SLA & Reporting**: Improvements to

Cited: CRM-14, CRM-18, CRM-22, CRM-25, CRM-27, CRM-35, CRM-39, CRM-43

q0076 · pagination-required · A:correct B:partial · Give me a complete breakdown of every issue in the CRM project, including their current status.
Expected keys: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-97, CRM-96, CRM-95 …(+94)
Expected count: 100
Oracle JQL: project = CRM

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 89.7s · 7 tool calls

The CRM project currently contains **100 issues**, all of which are in the **To Do** status. These issues are organized into four major strategic initiatives aimed at improving customer experience and operational efficiency.

### **Project Summary**
*   **Total Issues**: 100
*   **Status Breakdown**: 100% To Do
*   **Key Initiatives**: Billing Dispute Resolution, Account Recovery Modernization, Refund Processing Efficiency, and Technical Issue Reporting.

---

### **1. Billing Dispute Resolution (CRM-1 to CRM-25)**
This initiative focuses on reducing the volume and resolution time of billing disputes through automation and better customer self-service.
*   **[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**: Reduce Billing Dispute Resolution Time and Volume (Initiative Lead)
*   **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)**: Implement Automated Billing Dispute Triage System
*   **[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**: Create Customer Self-Service Billing Dispute Portal
*   **[CRM-11](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-11)**: Develop Proactive Billing Alert System for Unusual Charges
*   **[CRM-16](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-16)**: Streamline Internal Billing Adjustment Approval Workflow
*   **[CRM-21](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-21)**: Enhance Billing Statement Clarity and Transparency
*   *(Plus 19 supporting technical and design tasks)*

### **2. Account Recovery Modernization (CRM-26 to CRM-50)**
Aims to reduce the 20% of support volume caused by account access issues by implementing more secure and automated recovery methods.
*   **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**: Modernize Account Recovery Process to Reduce Support Volume (Initiative Lead)
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for Account Recovery
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: Build Automated Identity Verification Pipeline
*   **[CRM-36](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-36)**: Create Secure "Trusted Contact" Recovery Method
*   **[CRM-41](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-41)**: Implement Behavioral Biometrics for Risk-Based Recovery
*   **[CRM-46](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-46)**: Develop Account Recovery Health Dashboard
*   *(Plus 19 supporting technical and design tasks)*

### **3. Refund Processing Efficiency (CRM-51 to CRM-75)**
Focuses on automating refund eligibility and improvin

q0079 · pagination-required · A:correct B:partial · Retrieve all issues from the SMP project and summarize each one.
Expected keys: SMP-912, SMP-911, SMP-910, SMP-909, SMP-908, SMP-907 …(+904)
Expected count: 910
Oracle JQL: project = SMP

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 34.0s · 1 tool calls

The SMP project contains **910 issues** ranging from website quality audits and motorcycle mechanical reports to software development tasks for the Flet framework.

### **Project Overview**
*   **Quality Audits (Recent)**: Detailed audits for various web properties (Acme Print, Vistaprint clones).
*   **Motorcycle Maintenance**: A vast collection of mechanical issues across multiple brands (Yamaha, KTM, BMW, Ducati, Harley-Davidson, Royal Enfield, etc.), covering drivetrain, suspension, electrical, and engine problems.
*   **Software Development**: Technical tasks and bug reports related to the Flet framework, Flutter integration, and cross-platform deployment (Windows, macOS, iOS, Android).

---

### **Issue List (Recent to Oldest)**

1.  **[SMP-912](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-912)**: Quality Audit for Acme Print.
2.  **[SMP-911](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-911)**: Website Quality Audit: Acme Print - [CRITICAL] Multiple findings.
3.  **[SMP-910](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-910)**: Quality audit for Acme Print staging environment.
4.  **[SMP-909](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-909)**: Website Quality Audit Findings for Vistaprint.

q0091 · pagination-required · A:correct B:partial · List all issues between BUGS-1 and BUGS-100 with their descriptions.
Expected keys: BUGS-100, BUGS-99, BUGS-98, BUGS-97, BUGS-96, BUGS-95 …(+94)
Expected count: 100
Oracle JQL: project = BUGS AND key >= BUGS-1 AND key <= BUGS-100

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 70.6s · 3 tool calls

Here are the issues from **BUGS-1** to **BUGS-100** with their descriptions:

1. **[BUGS-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-1)**: Critical Security Vulnerability: SQL Injection in Search - A critical SQL injection vulnerability has been identified in the main search bar. An attacker can bypass authentication and extract sensitive user data by crafting specific search queries.
2. **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**: Fix SQL Injection in Search - Sanitize all user inputs in the search query using parameterized queries. Implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) rule to block common SQL injection patterns.
3. **[BUGS-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)**: Performance Degradation on Dashboard - The main dashboard is taking over 10 seconds to load for users with more than 50 active projects. This is due to inefficient database queries fetching all project details at once.
4. **[BUGS-4](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-4)**: Optimize Dashboard Queries - Implement pagination for the project list on the dashboard. Use caching for frequently accessed project metadata to reduce database load.
5. **[BUGS-5](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-5)**: Broken Link in Welcome Email - The 'Get Started' link in the new user welcome email points to a non-existent staging URL instead of the production environment.
6. **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**: Update Welcome Email Link - Change the 'Get Started' link in the welcome email template to point to the correct production URL. Verify the link in the staging environment before deployment.
7. **[BUGS-7](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-7)**: UI Glitch: Overlapping Text in Mobile View - On mobile devices, the issue description text overlaps with the sidebar menu when the screen is in portrait mode.
8. **[BUGS-8](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-8)**: Fix Mobile UI Overlap - Adjust the CSS media queries to ensure the issue description has proper padding and does not overlap with the sidebar on small screens.
9. **[BUGS-9](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-9)**: Missing Translation for 'Submit' Button - The 'Submit' button on the issue creation form is not translated into French, even when the user's language is set to French.
10. **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)**: Add French Translation for 'Submit' - Add the French translation for the 'Submit' button to the localization files. Ensure all other bu

Cited: BUGS-1, BUGS-10, BUGS-100, BUGS-11, BUGS-12, BUGS-13, BUGS-14, BUGS-15

q0100 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What are the common root causes behind API-related bugs in the BUGS project?
Expected themes: inconsistent timezone handling, datetime format inconsistency, rate limiter miscounting requests, REST API design inconsistencies

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 54.8s · 4 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the issues in the **BUGS** project, the common root causes behind API-related bugs and technical debt fall into several distinct categories:

### 1. Inconsistent Data Standards (Timezones & Dates)
The most prevalent technical issue is the lack of a unified standard for date and time representation across different endpoints.
*   **Root Cause:** Ad-hoc implementation where some endpoints use UTC, others use server local time, and some omit timezone indicators entirely.
*   **Impact:** This causes integration bugs for clients in different timezones and requires significant normalization effort.
*   **Key Issues:** **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)** (Inconsistent timezone handling), **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)** (Input normalization), and **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)** (Audit of response consistency).

### 2. Protocol-Level Logic Errors (Rate Limiting)
There is a specific recurring issue with how the API infrastructure handles browser-based security protocols.
*   **Root Cause:** The rate limiter incorrectly counts CORS `OPTIONS` preflight requests against the user's quota.
*   **Impact:** Users of Single Page Applications (SPAs) hit rate limits twice as fast as expected because every "real" request is preceded by a preflight request that consumes quota.
*   **Key Issue:** **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)** (Rate limiter counting preflights).

### 3. Structural Inconsistency (API Design Debt)
The "v1" API suffers from significant design drift and lack of a strict RESTful specification.
*   **Root Cause:** Inconsistent naming conventions (mixing singular and plural resources like `/issue` vs `/projects`) and varying methods for passing IDs (path vs. query parameters).
*   **Impact:** High developer friction and confusion, leading to increased support tickets.
*   **Key Issue:** **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)** (Inconsistent REST resource structure).

### 4. Platform-Specific Security Changes (Mobile APIs)
For mobile-facing APIs, bugs are frequently triggered by external OS updates.
*   **Root Cause:** Stricter security requirements in newer OS versions (e.g., Android 12+) and library updates (AndroidX Biometric) that break existing callback patterns.
*   **Impact:** Critical application crashes during sensitive flows like MFA enrollment.
*   **Key Issues:** **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/br

Cited: BUGS-100, BUGS-18, BUGS-20, BUGS-76, BUGS-77, BUGS-85, BUGS-93, BUGS-94

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes (inconsistent timezone handling, datetime format inconsistency, rate limiter miscounting requests, REST API design inconsistencies) with specific issue citations and well-organized root cause analysis, plus goes beyond with additional valid categories like mobile API issues and documentation gaps.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 6.2s · 2 tool calls

API-related bugs in BUGS share four recurring root causes: (1) inconsistent timezone/datetime handling across endpoints (BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100) with some fields in UTC, others in server-local time, others without TZ indicator; (2) rate limiter design flaws that count CORS OPTIONS preflight requests against quota, causing users to hit limits at half their actual request volume (BUGS-85, BUGS-86, BUGS-87); (3) inconsistent REST API design in v1 (mixed singular/plural, query vs path params, non-uniform error envelopes, mixed camel/snake_case) requiring a v2 redesign (BUGS-77, BUGS-76); (4) documentation drift / lack of OpenAPI source-of-truth and missing code examples / interactive console (BUGS-93, BUGS-94, BUGS-78). The unifying theme is accumulated v1 technical debt and missing standardization layers (datetime serializer, rate-limit middleware, OpenAPI spec).

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100, BUGS-85, BUGS-86, BUGS-87, BUGS-77

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected themes (timezone handling, datetime inconsistency, rate limiter miscounting, REST API design issues) with specific issue citations and provides additional relevant insights about documentation drift and technical debt.

q0101 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Analyze the mobile app issues in BUGS - what underlying technical problems are causing user-facing symptoms?
Expected themes: scroll performance jank / frame drops, excessive battery drain from background sync, network requests failing silently, rich text rendering on main thread

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 48.8s · 3 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the issues in the **BUGS** project, the mobile application is currently facing several critical technical challenges that directly impact stability, performance, and security.

The underlying technical problems can be categorized into six key areas:

### 1. Memory Management & Stability
*   **User Symptom**: Frequent app crashes, particularly when viewing attachments.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: The iOS app attempts to load large attachments (15MB+) entirely into memory before rendering a preview. This triggers `EXC_RESOURCE` memory exhaustion crashes, especially on devices with 2GB RAM like the iPhone 8 or SE.
*   **Key Issue**: **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)** (Accounts for 23% of all iOS crashes).

### 2. UI Thread Blocking (Performance Jank)
*   **User Symptom**: Noticeable stuttering and frame drops (15-20fps) while scrolling through issue lists.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: Several expensive operations are being performed on the main thread during scrolling:
    *   Rich text (Markdown) rendering.
    *   Synchronous loading of avatar images.
    *   Lack of caching for complex cell height calculations.
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**, **[BUGS-75](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-75)**.

### 3. Network Resilience & Offline Support
*   **User Symptom**: The app "hangs" indefinitely on poor connections; users cannot work without an active internet connection.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: 
    *   **Silent Failures**: On Android, network timeouts (OkHttp) are not handled at the UI layer, leaving loading spinners active indefinitely.
    *   **Lack of Persistence**: There is no local queue (Room/CoreData) to store "Pending" issues, meaning all creation logic requires a synchronous network response.
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**, **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)**, **[BUGS-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-69)**.

### 4. Background Resource Inefficiency
*   **User Symptom**: Excessive battery drain (15-20% daily) even when the app is not in use.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: 
    *   Background sync runs on a fixed 5-minute interval regardless of data changes.
    *   Legacy code is still requesting location services and maintaining active WebSockets in the background.
    *   Lack of battery-aware scheduling (e.g., `WorkManager` constraints or `BGAppRefreshTa

Cited: BUGS-14, BUGS-18, BUGS-20, BUGS-51, BUGS-52, BUGS-56, BUGS-60, BUGS-61

Judge: The answer provides an exceptionally thorough and well-organized analysis of mobile app technical problems, directly addressing all expected themes (scroll performance/frame drops, battery drain from background sync, network failures, rich text rendering on main thread) plus additional valid categories like memory management, navigation/deep linking, and security issues, all grounded with specific issue citations.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.4s · 1 tool calls

Mobile user-facing symptoms in BUGS map to specific underlying technical problems: (1) scroll jank and 15-20fps drops (BUGS-72) caused by markdown/rich-text rendering on the main thread, synchronous avatar image loads, uncached cell-height calculation, and 50+ live cells held in memory; (2) excessive battery drain (BUGS-60) caused by background sync running every 5 min regardless of settings, radio waking with no new data, an unused legacy location permission, and a WebSocket kept alive while backgrounded; (3) silent network failures on poor connections (BUGS-56) where OkHttp's 60s timeout is not surfaced to the UI, no retry/backoff, no offline detection; (4) iOS attachment crashes (BUGS-52) from loading entire 15MB+ files into memory instead of using QuickLook/streaming; (5) MFA crashes on Android 12+ (BUGS-18) due to biometric API version handling. Root themes: main-thread heavy rendering, no battery-aware scheduling, missing UX for transient/timeout failures, and unbounded memory loads.

Cited: BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56, BUGS-52, BUGS-18, BUGS-51, BUGS-61, BUGS-63

Judge: The answer provides excellent grounded analysis mapping user-facing symptoms to underlying technical causes, directly addressing all expected themes (scroll jank from main-thread rendering, battery drain from background sync, silent network failures) plus additional valid issues like memory management and API compatibility, with specific issue citations and root cause identification.

q0102 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Looking at CRM issues, what patterns indicate systemic gaps in the customer support workflow?
Expected themes: lack of refund status visibility, missing engineering escalation path, account lockout self-service gaps, incident-to-ticket linking missing

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 37.0s · 2 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the **CRM (Customer Support)** project issues, several systemic gaps in the current workflow have been identified. These gaps are currently being addressed through a series of modernization and automation initiatives:

### 1. High Manual Burden in Account Recovery
A significant portion of support volume appears to stem from manual account recovery and security lockouts.
*   **Gap**: Lack of self-service and automated security protocols.
*   **Evidence**: Issues like **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)** (Modernize Account Recovery to Reduce Volume) and **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** (Automated Account Lockout Detection) indicate that agents are currently the primary bottleneck for routine security tasks.
*   **Impact**: Increased ticket volume and slower resolution for users locked out of their accounts.

### 2. Inefficient Billing & Refund Workflows
The current process for handling disputes and refunds is largely manual and lacks integration with payment providers.
*   **Gap**: Absence of automated eligibility checks and direct API integrations.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-51)** (Improve Refund Processing Efficiency) and **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)** (Integrate with Stripe/PayPal APIs) suggest that agents are likely jumping between systems to process financial transactions.
*   **Impact**: High "Time to Resolve" for billing issues and increased risk of human error in refund processing.

### 3. Technical Context Deficit
Agents often lack the necessary technical data to troubleshoot issues on the first contact.
*   **Gap**: No automated collection of customer environment data or crash logs.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)** (Customer Technical Environment Collection) and **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)** (Enhance App Crash Reporting) highlight a need for better data at the point of ticket creation.
*   **Impact**: Excessive "back-and-forth" communication with customers to gather basic technical details (OS, app version, etc.).

### 4. Reactive vs. Proactive Support
The workflow is currently triggered almost exclusively by customer complaints rather than system alerts.
*   **Gap**: Lack of proactive monitoring for customer-facing failures (e.g., shipping delays, technical outages).
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)*

Cited: CRM-22, CRM-24, CRM-26, CRM-27, CRM-35, CRM-51, CRM-52, CRM-54

Judge: The answer provides excellent, well-structured analysis of systemic workflow gaps with specific issue citations covering account lockout self-service gaps, refund processing inefficiencies, escalation tracking issues, and proactive monitoring needs - directly addressing the spirit of the question with real data evidence.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 6.1s · 2 tool calls

CRM patterns reveal four systemic support-workflow gaps: (1) no customer-facing visibility into refund status, generating 15% of agent volume in 'where is my refund' follow-ups (CRM-56, CRM-57, CRM-58, CRM-59); (2) no formal engineering escalation path - reproducible bugs are tracked in spreadsheets, ad-hoc emails, and Slack mentions, averaging 2 weeks to acknowledgment (CRM-81, CRM-82, CRM-83, CRM-84); (3) account lockout self-service gaps - ~2,000 lockouts/day with no self-unlock, forcing email-to-support (CRM-35, CRM-37, CRM-38); (4) no incident-to-ticket linking, causing duplicate triage during outages (CRM-93, CRM-95, CRM-96). Underlying systemic issue: support, engineering, and incident-management tools are siloed; customer-facing transparency is missing.

Cited: CRM-56, CRM-81, CRM-35, CRM-93, CRM-95, CRM-96, CRM-82, CRM-83

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected themes with specific issue citations and quantified data: refund status visibility (15% agent volume), missing engineering escalation (2-week acknowledgment), account lockout self-service gaps (~2,000/day), and incident-to-ticket linking issues, plus identifies the underlying systemic cause of tool silos.

q0103 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What datetime and timezone issues exist across the BUGS project and what's the common technical root cause?
Expected keys: BUGS-100, BUGS-99, BUGS-98, BUGS-97
Expected count: 4
Oracle JQL: project = BUGS AND (summary ~ datetime OR summary ~ timezone OR description ~ "UTC")
Expected themes: inconsistent timezone handling, server timezone vs UTC, missing timezone indicator, ISO 8601 standardization needed

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 24.7s · 2 tool calls

The analysis of the **BUGS** project reveals a cluster of issues related to inconsistent datetime and timezone handling.

### Identified Issues
1. **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: **Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling for date fields**
   - This is the primary bug report detailing how different endpoints return dates in varying formats: some in UTC (correct), some in server local time (incorrect), and some without any timezone indicator at all (ambiguous).
2. **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: **Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency**
   - An initiative to map out all inconsistent fields and identify database columns that are incorrectly storing local time instead of UTC.
3. **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)**: **Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output**
   - A task to create a centralized JSON serializer to force all API outputs to follow the ISO 8601 standard with a `Z` (UTC) suffix.
4. **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)**: **Add input datetime normalization with deprecation warnings**
   - A task to ensure all incoming data (regardless of format) is normalized to UTC internally upon receipt.

### Common Technical Root Cause
The common technical root cause is the **absence of a unified datetime standard across the application's layers (API, Logic, and Database)**. 

Specifically, the system suffers from:
*   **Non-Standardized Storage**: Some database columns were configured to store "server time" rather than UTC, leading to data corruption when the server environment or timezone settings change.
*   **Decentralized Serialization**: Individual endpoints or services are likely using default or varied serialization logic rather than a single, global configuration, resulting in the mix of ISO 8601, raw strings, and local time formats.
*   **Ambiguous Input Parsing**: The system lacks a strict normalization layer at the API gateway/input level, allowing inconsistent formats to propagate into the internal logic.

Cited: BUGS-100, BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99

Judge: The answer comprehensively identifies datetime/timezone issues across the BUGS project, citing four relevant issues that directly address all expected themes (inconsistent timezone handling, server vs UTC, missing timezone indicators, ISO 8601 standardization), and provides a thorough technical root cause analysis grounded in the cited data.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.8s · 1 tool calls

Datetime/timezone issues in BUGS span four issues forming a single epic-style cluster: BUGS-97 (Story, High) 'Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling for date fields' is the parent problem - some endpoints return UTC with Z suffix, others return server-local time without indicator, date-only fields are TZ-ambiguous. Subtasks: BUGS-98 'Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency' (script all endpoints, identify columns storing local time needing migration), BUGS-99 'Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output', BUGS-100 'Add input datetime normalization with deprecation warnings'. Common technical root cause: absence of a centralized serializer/normalizer; datetimes are formatted ad-hoc in handlers and some database columns store server-local time, so UTC-vs-local divergence accumulated over time. Fix path is ISO 8601 standardization, X-Request-Timezone header for client rendering, and DB audit/migration of any local-time columns.

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses datetime/timezone issues with specific issue citations, identifies the exact technical root cause (absence of centralized serializer, ad-hoc formatting, local-time columns), and covers all expected themes including inconsistent timezone handling, UTC vs server timezone, missing timezone indicator, and ISO 8601 standardization as the solution path.

q0104 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Based on high priority issues in CRM, what are the main security and fraud-related concerns that need addressing?
Expected keys: CRM-85, CRM-81, CRM-60, CRM-56, CRM-35, CRM-31 …(+2)
Expected count: 8
Oracle JQL: project = CRM AND priority = High
Expected themes: refund fraud detection, account takeover prevention, identity verification gaps, login lockout handling

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 53.3s · 3 tool calls

Based on the high-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the main security and fraud-related concerns center around account security, refund fraud detection, and dispute management.

### 1. Refund Fraud Detection
There is a significant concern regarding refund fraud, with an estimated 4% fraud rate and less than half of it being caught.
*   **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: **Develop Refund Fraud Detection Model with Machine Learning**
    *   **Concern**: Patterns include "item-not-received" claims after delivery, serial refunders across multiple accounts, and the return of counterfeit items.
    *   **Action**: Implementing an ML model to score refund requests and routing high-risk cases (>75 score) to a specialist queue with a 48-hour hold.

### 2. Account Takeover (ATO) & Identity Verification
The project has identified a trend of account takeovers (12 incidents last quarter) where attackers change email addresses after phishing passwords.
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: **Create Identity Verification Workflow for High-Risk Account Changes**
    *   **Concern**: Unauthorized sensitive account changes (email/password).
    *   **Action**: Requiring re-authentication and confirmation from both old and new emails for changes, or government ID uploads if the old email is inaccessible.
*   **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**: **Develop Automated Account Lockout Detection**
    *   **Concern**: Legitimate users are frequently locked out due to failed logins, creating a support burden and potential security frustration.
    *   **Action**: Implementing clearer messaging and self-service unlocks for known devices.

### 3. Secure Account Recovery
Current recovery processes are manual and slow, which can lead to users being locked out of their accounts for days.
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: **Implement SMS-Based Account Recovery**
    *   **Concern**: Users who lose email access have no self-service way to recover accounts.
    *   **Action**: Adding SMS-based recovery with rate-limiting (3 attempts/hour) and 10-minute code expiration to balance security and accessibility.

### 4. Dispute & Evidence Management
Manual processes for handling billing disputes are leading to inconsistent data and slow resolution times, which can be exploited.
*   **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)**: **Automated Transaction Evidence Retrieval**
    *

Cited: CRM-2, CRM-27, CRM-31, CRM-35, CRM-6, CRM-60

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes with specific issue citations: refund fraud detection (CRM-60), account takeover prevention (CRM-31), identity verification gaps (CRM-31, CRM-27), and login lockout handling (CRM-35), plus additional relevant security concerns around dispute management.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.5s · 1 tool calls

High-priority CRM issues cluster around four security/fraud concerns: (1) Refund fraud - estimated 4% rate, catching <50%; CRM-60 builds an ML model with 0-100 risk scoring, fraud-specialist queue for >75 scores, and feature inputs covering customer history, claim type, and device fingerprint; CRM-6 builds the self-service dispute portal with rate-limit (max 3/24h) abuse controls. (2) Account takeover prevention - 12 ATO incidents last quarter from phished passwords + email changes; CRM-31 mandates re-auth + dual confirmation for email changes, government-ID fallback via Jumio, and audit trail visible to support. (3) Account lockout / credential stuffing handling - CRM-35 keeps 30-min lockout for suspicious patterns (multi-IP, VPN/TOR ranges) while adding self-unlock for legitimate users. (4) Multi-transaction billing dispute escalation - CRM-10 detects related subscription charges, requires supervisor approval >$200, and adds compliance audit log. Supporting: CRM-56 (refund tracking), CRM-81 (engineering escalation), CRM-85 (automated troubleshooting). Core theme: balancing friction reduction for legitimate users with stronger verification at high-risk moments.

Cited: CRM-60, CRM-31, CRM-35, CRM-10, CRM-6, CRM-56, CRM-81, CRM-85

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected security/fraud themes (refund fraud detection via CRM-60/CRM-6, account takeover prevention via CRM-31, identity verification via Jumio integration, and login lockout handling via CRM-35) with specific issue keys, quantified metrics, and detailed implementation approaches grounded in real CRM data.

ReadTimeout:

Cited: —

Judge: runner failed: ReadTimeout:

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.7s · 2 tool calls

CRM integration/alerting gaps that delay response: (1) No PagerDuty integration for support-detected emerging issues - CRM-100 plans P1 PagerDuty + P2 Slack alerts when ticket volume exceeds baseline. (2) No real-time ticket-volume monitoring or anomaly detection - CRM-98 builds a streaming pipeline with 5-min window aggregation segmented by category/app version/OS/geo; CRM-99 implements baseline-comparison anomaly detection; CRM-97 raises engineering PagerDuty when ticket volume exceeds 2x baseline (recent example: Android 14 login failures detected via tickets 4 hours before monitoring saw API errors because failures were client-side). (3) No incident auto-creation from PagerDuty alerts tagged 'customer-facing' (CRM-95), and no ticket-to-incident linking for duplicate prevention (CRM-96). (4) No public status page (CRM-93/CRM-94) so customers don't see known issues, flooding support with duplicates. Root cause: support telemetry runs in isolation from engineering/SRE alerting stack.

Cited: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-97, CRM-95, CRM-96, CRM-93

q0106 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Examine API documentation issues in BUGS - what's causing developer confusion based on the issue descriptions?
Expected themes: missing code examples, tutorial guides needed, OpenAPI documentation setup, inconsistent endpoint naming

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 42.9s · 3 tool calls

Based on an examination of the issues in the **BUGS** project, developer confusion regarding the API and its documentation stems from several key areas of inconsistency and technical debt:

### 1. Inconsistent Data Formats & Timezones
*   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Developers are confused by inconsistent timezone handling. Some endpoints return UTC, others return server time without indicators, and some return ambiguous date-only strings. This leads to bugs in third-party integrations that rely on precise timing.

### 2. Outdated and Static Documentation
*   **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**: The current documentation is a static Markdown file that is frequently out of sync with the actual API implementation. This forces developers to use "trial-and-error" or open support tickets to understand how endpoints actually behave.
*   **[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**: General "poor documentation" is cited as a primary hurdle for third-party integrations, contributing to a high volume of support tickets.

### 3. Structural Inconsistencies (REST Design)
*   **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**: The API lacks a consistent resource structure. Developers struggle with:
    *   **Naming**: Mixing singular and plural endpoints (e.g., `/issue/{id}` vs `/projects/{id}`).
    *   **Parameters**: Inconsistent use of path parameters vs. query parameters for IDs.
    *   **Error Responses**: Error formats vary wildly between endpoints, making it difficult to write consistent error-handling logic.

### 4. Unexpected Rate Limiting Behavior
*   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: The rate limiter incorrectly counts CORS `OPTIONS` preflight requests against a user's quota. This causes developers to hit limits twice as fast as expected, especially in Single Page Applications (SPAs). Additionally, the absence of rate limit headers makes it impossible for developers to programmatically manage their request pacing.

### 5. Latency in System State (Search Indexing)
*   **[BUGS-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-39)**: A 30-60 second delay in search indexing causes "freshness" confusion. Developers/users create an issue and immediately search for it; when it doesn't appear, they assume the API call failed and often create duplicate records.

### 6. Missing Modern Features
*   **[BUGS-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-81)** & **[BU

Cited: BUGS-39, BUGS-76, BUGS-77, BUGS-81, BUGS-85, BUGS-89, BUGS-93, BUGS-97

Judge: The answer provides a comprehensive, well-organized analysis of API documentation issues causing developer confusion, citing specific issues with concrete details about inconsistent naming (matching expected themes), outdated docs, timezone handling, rate limiting, and missing features - all grounded in real issue data with proper citations.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.3s · 1 tool calls

API documentation issues in BUGS show developer confusion stems from: (1) static Markdown docs that drift out of sync with implementation, forcing devs into trial-and-error and support tickets (BUGS-93); (2) lack of an OpenAPI source-of-truth - BUGS-78 creates the OpenAPI 3.1 spec for v2 with $ref reusable components and validation, BUGS-94 deploys Stoplight/Redocly auto-updated on spec changes; (3) missing code examples in multiple languages (Python/JS/Ruby/Go/cURL) and no interactive 'try it' console (BUGS-93); (4) inconsistent endpoint naming, error response formats, and pagination across v1 (BUGS-77) compounds doc confusion since the API itself isn't predictable. Underlying cause: docs are a manual artifact rather than generated, and the v1 API design itself lacks the consistency that makes docs scannable. v2 modernization (BUGS-76) bundles API consistency + spec-driven docs + dev portal as a unit.

Cited: BUGS-93, BUGS-94, BUGS-78, BUGS-77, BUGS-76

Judge: The answer thoroughly addresses API documentation issues causing developer confusion, covering all expected themes (missing code examples, OpenAPI setup, inconsistent endpoint naming) with specific issue citations and grounded analysis of root causes including static docs drifting out of sync and lack of spec-driven documentation.

q0107 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What common technical debt patterns can be identified across high priority bugs in the BUGS project?
Expected keys: BUGS-97, BUGS-85, BUGS-77, BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56 …(+4)
Expected count: 10
Oracle JQL: project = BUGS AND priority = High
Expected themes: inconsistent API design, rate limiter bugs, mobile performance issues, network error handling gaps

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 62.7s · 5 tool calls

Based on an analysis of high-priority bugs in the **BUGS** project, several recurring technical debt patterns have emerged. These patterns indicate underlying architectural weaknesses and "quick fixes" that are now impacting stability and performance.

### 1. Resource Management & Memory Leaks
A significant number of crashes and performance issues stem from improper handling of system resources.
*   **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**: Memory leaks in the dashboard caused by orphaned subscriptions that aren't cleaned up on unmount.
*   **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**: iOS crashes due to loading large attachments (15MB+) entirely into memory rather than using streaming or tiled previews.
*   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: UI "jank" caused by expensive, uncached cell height calculations and synchronous image loading on the main thread.

### 2. Concurrency & State Synchronization
Race conditions and initialization timing issues are causing critical failures in authentication and navigation.
*   **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**: A race condition in the OAuth flow where multiple browser tabs simultaneously attempt to refresh tokens, leading to unexpected logouts.
*   **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)**: Deep links on Android fail because the handler executes before the navigation stack is fully initialized, a classic "order of operations" debt.

### 3. Legacy Code & Outdated Implementations
Older code paths and rigid implementations are failing to meet modern requirements or library updates.
*   **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**: Excessive battery drain caused by legacy code that requests location services it no longer uses and an inefficient 5-minute background sync.
*   **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**: Biometric crashes occurring because the existing implementation didn't account for the stricter security requirements of newer AndroidX libraries.
*   **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)**: An overly rigid SAML parser that rejects valid enterprise attributes, indicating a "security-first" implementation that lacks the flexibility required for real-world integrations.

### 4. Inconsistent API & Data Standards
Lack of unified standards across the backend is creating friction for integrations and frontend logic.
*   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/brows

Cited: BUGS-10, BUGS-18, BUGS-2, BUGS-43, BUGS-52, BUGS-56, BUGS-6, BUGS-60

Judge: Excellent analytical answer that identifies 5 distinct technical debt patterns (Resource Management, Concurrency, Legacy Code, Inconsistent API/Data Standards, Fragile Error Handling) with specific issue citations, directly addressing expected themes like inconsistent API design, rate limiter bugs, mobile performance issues, and network error handling gaps with grounded evidence from the BUGS project.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.0s · 1 tool calls

High-priority BUGS issues reveal a consistent technical-debt picture across 10 tickets: API-design debt - inconsistent v1 REST design (BUGS-77), datetime/timezone inconsistency (BUGS-97), rate limiter counting OPTIONS preflight (BUGS-85); mobile-platform debt - scroll-perf jank from main-thread rich-text rendering (BUGS-72), excessive battery drain from undisciplined background sync (BUGS-60), silent network failures on Android with no retry/timeout UX (BUGS-56), MFA crash from missing Android 12+ biometric API handling (BUGS-18); auth/session debt - session invalidation not propagating to mobile after password change (BUGS-14), memory leak in auth state subscription (BUGS-6); search-relevance debt - scoring ignores severity/recency (BUGS-35). Common pattern: missing centralized layers (serializer, rate-limit middleware, network/UX layer, push-based session sync) and accumulated platform-API drift that wasn't kept current.

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-85, BUGS-77, BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56, BUGS-35, BUGS-18

Judge: The answer provides excellent grounded analysis identifying multiple technical debt patterns (API design inconsistency, mobile platform debt, auth/session debt, search relevance debt) with specific issue citations, directly matching expected themes like API design, rate limiter bugs, mobile performance, and network error handling while also surfacing additional valid patterns from the real data.

q0108 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Looking at CRM subtasks, what operational inefficiencies are the team trying to solve?
Expected keys: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-96, CRM-95, CRM-94 …(+66)
Expected count: 72
Oracle JQL: project = CRM AND issuetype = Subtask
Expected themes: ticket-to-incident linking, PagerDuty integration, anomaly detection baseline, status page evaluation

Gemini 2.5 + Custom MCP · correct · 94.1s · 5 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the subtasks within the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, the team is focused on solving several key operational inefficiencies through automation, self-service, and improved data integration:

### 1. Manual Billing Dispute & Evidence Collection
Currently, agents appear to be manually gathering data for billing disputes, which is time-consuming and prone to error.
*   **Solution**: The team is building an automated evidence compilation pipeline.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-3)**: Designing a standardized evidence packet schema and PDF template.
    *   **[CRM-4](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-4)**: Integrating the PaymentService API for automatic transaction retrieval.
    *   **[CRM-5](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-5)**: Creating background jobs to compile evidence automatically when a ticket is created.
    *   **[CRM-11](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-11)**: Implementing a clustering algorithm to handle complex subscription disputes.

### 2. High Support Volume for Account Recovery
A significant portion of support volume likely stems from users locked out of their accounts, requiring manual identity verification by agents.
*   **Solution**: Implementing self-service recovery tools and automated ID verification.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-33](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-33)**: Integrating Jumio for automated government ID verification.
    *   **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**: Building a self-service account unlock flow.
    *   **[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)** & **[CRM-45](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-45)**: Developing a backup code system to allow users to recover accounts without agent help.

### 3. Inefficient Refund & Return Workflows
The current process for refunds and returns involves manual eligibility checks and manual label generation.
*   **Solution**: Automating the decision-making and execution of refunds and returns.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-53](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-53)**: Building an eligibility rules engine to automate refund approvals.
    *   **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)**: Integrating Stripe/PayPal APIs for programmatic refund processing.
